raw_payload.author: Dragava
raw_payload.content:
Granted it's good, and I like most of PTA's films, but it's not like this is anywhere near as brilliant as something like There Will Be Blood or The Master.

The plot is very simple, and while that's not a bad thing, I found it difficult finding anything substantial to grab onto. The performances are certainly good but again it feels like all the actors with the exception of probably Chase Infiniti and maybe Sean Penn aren't given a whole lot to do.

In my opinion this was far less interestingly photographed than something like Phantom Thread, and with less compelling characters than something like The Master, and lacked the spectacle and gravitas of something like There Will Be Blood.

Still better than most films releasing these days, but if you're looking at all the rave reviews like I was and expecting a masterpiece, from my perspective I'd be lowering my expectations.

It gets a 7/10 for being well performed and pretty well written, but I can't go beyond that because it lacked the depth I'd normally be seeking from filmmakers of PTA's caliber.

raw_payload.author: PimpinAinttEasy
raw_payload.content:
Pales on second watch. Much less satisfying after I watched it for the second time today. The entertaining bits like Bob forgetting the password, Lockjaw's desperation to be hired by the Christmas Adventurers and the car chase seemed gimmicky after a revisit. It's definitely an overrated film. Maybe one of the better films of recent times. But pales in comparison to the great American classics of even the 90s. Don't even compared it to films from the 60s and the 70s. Generational masterpiece? No way.

One Battle After Another does capture the spirit of Vineland and Denis Jonson's Already Dead in the second half with its emphasis on the vastness of America and its roads and small towns in which there is a fair battle between good and evil and good may triumph.

The first half was more intense than the action packed second half.

Vineland, the book this movie is based on had a deeper understanding of the attraction to fascism with Prairie (Willa in the book) seeking out Brock Vond (Lockjaw) who might be her dad. One Battle After Another was more one dimensional with Lockjaw portrayed as a total wannabe climber and Willa unwilling to acknowledge him. This was really the film's weak point.

The film is unquestionably entertaining and gripping. But ultimately a bit flimsy and I felt like it did not amount to much in the end. I liked it enough to book tickets for a second time. It is one of the better movie hall experiences of the 21st century for me.

The performances, apart from Benicio Del Toro were nothing to write home about. Di Caprio looks like a wrinkled 18-year old who did not bother to shave to look like an adult. The great Sean Penn delivers an over the top performance that works in some scenes. You did feel bad for Lockjaw in the end so maybe it worked. Teyana Taylor and Chase Infiniti are badass but their characters are so one dimensional.

raw_payload.author: MindfulMovieBuff

raw_payload.author: luqman96

raw_payload.author: Katiegoldberg
raw_payload.content:
I saw 'One Battle After Another' last night, and I'm still not sure I get the hype. On paper, the film has everything: social commentary, action, radical politics, immigration, parenthood, even extremism. It tries to juggle so many weighty ideas at once - and I do applaud that - it's ambitious, topical, clearly trying to stir the pot. But ambition alone doesn't make for satisfying cinema, and this one ends up messy.

At nearly three hours long, it's a slog when you still don't really know the people you're meant to care about. Not a single character feels grounded - Benicio del Toro aside (and even then, his screen time is frustratingly short). The rest make decisions that have zero logic in the absence of backstory, leaving them feeling exaggerated and hollow. So many talented actors, yet I wasn't rooting for anyone. The "big message" moments - racism, political division, systemic power, identity - land far too on the nose, skimming the surface without ever cutting deep.

If I'm being fair, one thing I did love was the soundtrack - tense, pulsing, and full of nervous energy, it injects more urgency than the script ever does. But pace and tone overall are uneven. PTA's usual offbeat humour is basically MIA, and without it, long stretches drag and feel oddly dull. The film indulges its own grandeur, with sprawling chases and spectacle, but without clarity or cohesion. Honestly? It's his weakest film yet. It wants to be grand, relevant, provocative - and in parts it succeeds - but just doesn't hold it together. In the end, there's no clear overall message, just a lot of battles, one after another, that feel thrown at you without being stitched into something coherent.

raw_payload.author: nirvana_state_of_mind
raw_payload.content:
The title of my review is automatically going to be downvoted but it just represents exactly how I feel. This was a perfectly serviceable film that I was able to watch from start to finish and come out saying "yeah it was fine."

When it comes to any Paul Thomas Anderson film digging for a point and meaning to them is in my opinion a waste of time. You either connect with the characters and the surreal world that they live in, or you don't. Here I just didn't, I don't understand anything about what motivates the characters and only see surface level topics around immigration, racism and political revolution. For a PTA film the black humor is mostly absent, it is actually mostly played straight with a simple plot and nowhere near as much action as they are saying there is. I don't know who this was really made for because it seems too strange for wide audiences and too vanilla for PTA fans. Obviously some people say they love it and I'm not taking that away from you. But I fully expect the ratings on this film to normalize to an average film rating. If I'm wrong I'm glad audiences got something out of it.

raw_payload.author: Chris_Ego
raw_payload.content:
What is this movie about? It starts with the "French 75", a group with ideolical goals. Their rebellion changes nothing and their members soon end up in jail. This is portrayed without obvious moral underlining. The other half of the Paul Thomas Anderson movie is a family drama. That's also when the video editor decided to continue his work on an old VCR device. We get to see intense emotions, wild car chases and a few comedic performances.

At the end there is a hardly character devolpment to be witnessed. One of the main characters pretty much stays the same.

It's a shame, that this film lacks a clear continuity storywise. The formula follows the modern storytelling: intense emotions replace a stunning script. Of course, the cinematography is high standard, outstanding and well crafted. Yet the movie was not convincing. No rewatch planned. Try harder next time.

raw_payload.author: jannisheitkamp-90629
raw_payload.content:
I find the film strange and the story rather pointless.

From the very beginning, I kept waiting for some kind of deeper meaning or at least a surprising twist.

The acting, however, was very good - the cast managed to convey emotions convincingly, and their performances were clearly the strongest aspect of the movie. Unfortunately, strong acting alone cannot save a story that feels directionless.

In the end, the film seemed like an ambitious attempt that failed because of its weak script. The visuals and the performances might appeal to some viewers, but for me the whole experience was overshadowed by the fact that the story simply didn't make sense and felt like complete nonsense.

raw_payload.author: errorist
raw_payload.content:
Seeing rave reviews pop up everywhere, and this movie hitting the top 30 of all time in no time, I stayed away from reading anything - no reviews, no trailers - and just went in not knowing at all what it was about. The bar was set high, so I came expecting the masterpiece it was supposedly meant to be.

Now, I can already predict this will be a favorite at the Oscars, and let's be fair, that's not necessarily because it's an epic film (it's really just okay...), but because of the message it carries. In times of global turmoil, it's understandable that certain themes resonate more strongly, but it does feel like the movie's reception leans heavily on its political stance.

We're asked to sympathize with a left-wing radical group framed as misunderstood idealists, while the white men chasing them are portrayed as unsettling "pure white" supremacists. It's a very deliberate contrast, one that seems more designed to strike a chord with the current climate than to build genuine complexity.

I guess my point is: this movie had a head start because of its topic. The hype feels driven more by the message than by the actual quality of the film.

raw_payload.author: theo_karaeng
raw_payload.content: I really liked one battle after another.. it is fun, fast, and genuinely funny, with a chaotic energy that keeps pulling you forward.. the pacing and music create constant tension, and at times it reminded me of birdman or the unexpected virtue of ignorance, especially in how the soundtrack never lets you fully relax.. one of the most striking parts is the portrayal of the police.. they are shown as blatantly corrupt, almost absurd, but it feels intentional.. the movie uses exaggeration as satire, turning corruption into dark comedy.. you laugh at how extreme it is, then realize the ideas behind it are not completely unrealistic.. the story also quietly talks about family, especially the role of a father to a child.. there is a sense of responsibility, protection, and emotional presence that matters even in the middle of chaos.. at the same time, the movie does not shy away from showing that a mean mother do exist too, which makes the family dynamic feel messy and real, rather than idealized.. despite touching on heavy themes like abuse of power and broken families, the movie never becomes preachy or exhausting.. it keeps things entertaining first, letting the message sit quietly underneath the humor and chaos..

raw_payload.author: tynoel
raw_payload.content:
Paul Thomas Anderson's One Battle After Another is a film that wants to be epic but ends up didactic. There's no denying its technical accomplishments: the cinematography is vivid, almost painterly at times, and Leonardo DiCaprio and Sean Penn deliver performances that are magnetic, commanding your attention even when the story falters.

And falter it does. The film's ambition - to comment on political extremism - is undercut by its simplistic moral universe. One side of the conflict is portrayed as entirely evil, with no redeeming qualities or relatability. Every action they take is chaotic, violent, or manipulative, leaving the audience with no one to sympathize with on that side. This lack of nuance turns the movie from a story into a political lecture, making it feel less like cinema and more like ideological instruction.

In a moment when America feels more divided than ever, the timing of such a film is jarring. Its portrayal of chaos and extremism is so stark that it risks being taken too seriously by audiences at either extreme, rather than prompting reflection. More troublingly, it underscores how out of touch Hollywood can be with most Americans, presenting a world of ideological extremes that few viewers would recognize in real life.

Still, the film isn't without merit. Anderson's eye for detail, his visual inventiveness, and the sheer force of DiCaprio and Penn's performances make it watchable even when it preaches. But for a director capable of deep, character-driven storytelling, this attempt at political commentary feels like a missed opportunity.

raw_payload.author: li0904426
raw_payload.content:
The movie "One Battle After Another", in summary, is about a former revolutionary on a mission to save his missing daughter. Directed and co-written by Paul Thomas Anderson, it's a thrilling political action flick that really shows off, once again, his talent for both writing and directing. However, the movie isn't consistent in its construction; at times it's unconvincing, and other times it feels unfocused. More than once, I had the same reaction I did with last year's "Emilia Pérez": a wildly original story that often seems to jump around without much logic, where anything can happen.

The whole movie basically belongs to Sean Penn as Col. Lockjaw. He totally steals the show with a character who's creepy, unhinged, and downright disturbing.

Like "Emilia Pérez", it's one of those stories you're either into from the start or you just don't care about at all.

raw_payload.author: lotheravanti

Don't take the trailer for what this movie is. It is NOT an action movie. It is a pure character driven movie where pretty much ALL the characters shine. They are portrayed as very human, very flawed, not caricatures or tropes.

I'd say what stood out for me the most was Sean Penn's performance. Dude absolutely nails it. I've never thought of Sean Penn as intimidating, but he nails it here.

If you're expecting DiCaprio to go in guns blazing saving everyone, this ain't it. His performance is also TOP notch here as a washed-out has been pretty much powerless in the face of a crushing adversary. Yet he still decides to face everything head on.

Like Caught Stealing is pure Aronofsky and not an action movie, so too is One Battle After Another a pure Paul Joseph Anderson flick.

raw_payload.author: Pastoro
raw_payload.content:
Today I watched *one battle after another*. I had read all the glowing reviews beforehand, and I knew it was being praised as one of the best films of the year... but if I can give you one piece of advice: if you're going to see it, go in with no expectations-and avoid reading other people's opinions first.

Everyone kept saying it's an incredible film, probably the best of the year... I really wish I could've seen it through their eyes.

Personally, it didn't make me feel much of anything. Sure, there are moments that make you laugh, and it does offer some solid social commentary on what's going on in the U. S. right now. But I was expecting something with a powerful emotional punch, some great action, and a brilliant script-something that would leave a lasting impression. That didn't happen for me at all. I think this is the kind of movie you either love or don't connect with-no middle ground.

Visually, it's stunning. And with a cast like that, it was clear the performances would be strong.

In short: don't let even the best reviews shape your opinion. Go with your own perspective, and trust your own taste.

raw_payload.author: Daniil_Shadow
raw_payload.content:
Battle after another is a solid film with a lot of strong points. It's generally warm and even has a bit of light raunchiness at the start, yet it keeps you engaged. Leonardo DiCaprio's performance is outstanding-he plays an ordinary guy thrown into tough situations with incredible sincerity and conviction. Benicio Del Toro, though in a smaller role, adds charisma as well. The female characters, unfortunately, feel pretty shallow and lack depth.

The plot is simple but enjoyable, though there are minor logical gaps. The main downside is the antagonists: some feel too overpowered, while others seem weak, and the motives of many minor villains remain unclear. At times, their actions come off as clichéd, which slightly reduces the dramatic tension.

Overall, the film works really well-it's fresh, engaging, and visually impressive. It reminded me of Fargo, both the film and the series. If you like Leonardo DiCaprio or the Coen brothers, this movie is definitely worth watching.

raw_payload.author: redban02
raw_payload.content:
Alright, I went to see it. I just got back. Long story short: I'm a little disappointed, honestly.

I'll start with the good: the movie has some aesthetic scenes, particularly near the end. I like the desert scenery, and there are scenes inside a beautiful convent. There is a really cool, well-filmed car-chase. There are a few funny moments, like when Dicaprio yells at a phone operator (the previews spoiled a bit of this part though). Sean Penn's character offers a couple laughs too. Lastly - I really like Benicio Del Toro's character. He is the most charming and likable character in the movie.

In the end, though - I think the bad outweigh the positives. My first complaint is that there is so much unnecessary music throughout the entire movie. Even during some thrilling, beautiful chase scenes, the movie will have all sorts of jazz or piano music blaring, which got so annoying.

Anyways - I also found the movie to be quite slow and kinda dull. There's a ton of talk; there's a bit of fluff. More than once, I found myself yawning. I legit felt my eyes closing during the first 30-40 minutes of the movie, which focused a lot on Dicaprio's wife (who is a dry and loathsome character). Until the point where Sean Penn begins chasing Dicaprio and his daughter, the movie is a slog to get through. In some ways, the movie unfortunately reminded me a bit of "Black Bag," another critically-acclaimed movie that I didn't like much

The movie trips on itself by giving some critical moments to minor characters. To explain those parts would be to spoil, so I won't say much. The effect is that scenes that could have been hard-hitting end up falling flat.

The main protagonists are not likable. The movie mistakenly expects the viewer to find Dicaprio and his wife likable just because they free some detained illegal immigrants at the opening. In truth, I was actually pulling for Sean Penn's character until the movie randomly connects him to some white supremacist group. And speaking of that group - the movie is so vague about them. I knew that this group was a white supremacist group because I had read a few critic reviews. If I had not, then I wouldn't have even known that the group is supposed to be racist

The movie has a 98% on RT, and people are already calling it an Oscar winner. I feel that praise is overblown.

6.0/10.

raw_payload.author: QAgirl
raw_payload.content:
I'll admit, this didn't grab me at first. The opening felt slow, and the revolutionaries were a little too over the top for my taste. But somewhere along the way, the film won me over, mostly thanks to Bob. I've never been a big DiCaprio fan, yet he's fantastic here as a paranoid, anxious ex revolutionary who's clearly let himself go. He stumbles through scenes with this jittery, washed out energy, like a man who's spent far too many years drunk, high, and avoiding reality.

His phone calls with the revolution's customer service line where he can't remember the code he desperately needs, had me laughing out loud. Moments like that reminded me how rare it is to see something this original anymore.

The film throws everything into the mix: revolutionaries, white supremacist secret societies, hidden tunnels, explosions, political tension, bursts of action, and a healthy dose of absurdity. It's chaotic, strange, and surprisingly entertaining once it finds its rhythm.

raw_payload.author: JoshuaMercott
raw_payload.content:
Gripping and fascinating don't come close to describing this movie. "One Battle After Another" takes what appears to be a simple storyline and branches it out into a redemption arc involving revolution, protection, unity, and a complex shade of parenthood.

Leonardo DiCaprio as Bob and Sean Penn as Col. Steven J. Lockjaw give extraordinary performances. They make every frame in which they feature stand strong.

Teyana Taylor as Perfidia is a special highlight in the movie. Her portrayal is equal parts ferocious, conflicted, and vulnerable.

She and Bob (DiCaprio) experience complex character dynamics in this movie, which is remarkable in itself. On a related note, Lockjaw (Penn) and Sensei Sergio St. Carlos (Benicio Del Toro) capture unexpected character quirks that further enrich this script.

Notable moments from, Regina Hall as Deandra, Paul Grimstad as Sommerville, Chase Infiniti as Charlene/Willa, Tony Goldwyn as Virgil Throckmorton, and April Grace as Sister Rochelle.

"One Battle After Another" adapts the book "Vineland" by Thomas Pynchon (published 1990). I haven't read it, but this 2025 movie adaptation has given me more than a few reasons to do so.

Before long, our core characters' actions return to threaten their lives and livelihoods. They're part of a vigilante group known as the French 75, who were once (and still are, in a way) anti-prejudice revolutionaries.

This script, like the book from which it draws, is most certainly political. It sends a clear message about where the USA is headed as a country.

Remember, these are Trump-time immigration woes, distinct from the original novel - which is set in the 80s and focuses on the aftermath of radical movements from the 1960s to 70s.

A brief sexual undercurrent complements an otherwise racially charged plotline, making "One Battle After Another" feel both realistic and credible. And no, it's not based on actual events.

After Perfidia gets arrested during one of her revolutionary activities, she is later compelled to turn coat and reveal names. The movie takes a dark - familiar, rather, for a lot of Americans - turn from there.

More than sixteen years later, Bob and Perfidia's daughter Charlene - played notably by Chase Infiniti - has grown up. But the past, as we all know, doesn't keep to itself.

The movie exceptionally, and truthfully, captures the flaws in people - particularly those considered to be heroes. It shows how bad decisions made by good people can still create a giant mess.

The movie adequately covers the rising tide of xenophobia in the US, including ongoing problems with detentions and deportations.

As political thrillers go, this one packs a relevant punch. Though all its events are US-centric, the message is global... Revolution is often the way to make the powerful pay attention to what everyday people are going through.

raw_payload.author: daanjvanderhoeven
raw_payload.content:
Leonardo DiCaprio has somewhat slipped from my memory, but with this film he absolutely shines.

After playing a somewhat sad dummy in Once Upon a Time in Hollywood, in this movie DiCaprio proves himself as a terrific actor again. In the role of Bob he perfectly embodies a sympathetic loser and absolutely contributes to the thrilling course of the story.

Next to the great acting of not only DiCaprio and even BETTER Teyana Taylor, this movie paints an uncanny and utterly realistic ongoing? Or future scenario for the beautiful country we know as the United States of America.

raw_payload.author: TJ_at_the_drive_in
raw_payload.content: I didn't care for the opening scenes but after 20 or 30 minutes I thought it got significantly better. Not a great opening, very uneven compared to the rest. The film as a whole gets a lot better the longer you stick with it. The acting is the main reason my score is so high. Leo is great in this and so is Sean Penn. With all the nominations for benicio I thought he was gonna have a bigger performance but he's relegated to a significant side character. The action is good and the cinematography looks great. The dialogue is interesting. I just don't think its as great as everybody says it is. I had a good time for the most part. Its a little long but after that beginning the pace moves a lot smoother. There's not much explanation. Like we are told all of these things but are not told why to think this way or given reasons why we should think said way. Its a straight forward plot and I see why people like it so much. I think its a movie for the times and that's the reasons for the high scores but in the future years from now I don't think it will age as well as other films being released currently. I just think its a really good film I don't understand the glowing reviews calling it a masterpiece or winning a significant number of awards compared to other current greats. The character development is pretty thin. We are told these people are this and these people are that..not exactly sure what the message is supposed to be at the end of the day. Are they good guys? Are they bad guys? Morally grey? Its basically a story about a group of people trying to start a revolution. I like the story but don't try to think about it too much as you watch it just consume. Its heavily political so you'll likely love this or hate it. I'm leaning towards really liking it a lot I just think its overrated.

raw_payload.author: juicyj4113

Extra half star for the score, without that, this would not have hit as hard.

raw_payload.author: kikiru
raw_payload.content: People are calling this film Anderson's masterpiece. I can't agree. It's watchable and even good, but it would benefit from some serious editing as the last half really begins to drag. Pro Tip: The big chase scene in a feature film shouldn't drag! Over-the-top characters are so plentiful that it's almost a relief when one isn't (I would have loved to have seen more of Benicio Del Toro's sensei!). Those films, too, have plenty of over-the-top characters, but their stories are just more compelling.

raw_payload.author: petertwonine
raw_payload.content:
I came into One Battle After Another with no expectations, but usually anything with DiCaprio is good, if not fantastic. I had read some online hype and imagined something quite different, more of an action drama, along the lines of Taken, but with DiCaprio's magic. Instead, I got a messy, incoherent film that feels like a random jumble of ideas with no clear purpose.

In fact, this might be one of Leonardo DiCaprio's weakest performances. I honestly can't imagine why he even signed on for it, maybe he owed some of his SJW friends a favor? The script is a nonsensical steaming dogpile pulled out of a hat, and the movie offers no real payoff. By the end, you're left not caring about any of the characters, and what's meant to be social commentary comes off as banal and forgettable. I regret the time I wasted on it.

raw_payload.author: judebd
raw_payload.content:
This film is markedly overrated and does not merit a flawless 10/10 rating. While DiCaprio delivers a compelling and nuanced performance that elevates the production, the same cannot be said for his co-lead.

Penn's portrayal comes across as overly mechanical, lacking emotional depth and spontaneity. His character often feels robotic and misguided, which undermines the film's credibility and renders significant portions unintentionally absurd.

raw_payload.author: paulwigglesworth
raw_payload.content: This is an old school attempt at storytelling channeling the 1970s era of character driven movies like Serpico while adding a 1960s flair for inventive cinematography. The story is essentially nonsense pretending to be timely and relevant while the characters spiralling through bouts of personal and pseudo intellectual extremism are the real focus. The film presents extreme characters who are both foolish and admirable for believing in anything in an era where societal values and politics have trivialized previously serious aspects of the human experience. The film meanders and could have used more cutting but clearly it's a film that fell in love with character performances while forgetting the trivial plot. There's homages to Hitchcock (North by Northwest aspect of one character chasing while being chased), Spielberg (the car chasing scenes in the desert stir memories of the film Duel), Kubrick (Dr Stranglove's surreal political commentaries). The film stands apart from the mainstream and is a facsimile of greater movies but certainly isn't a classic - more of an oddball zinger with some mild attempt at social political commentary. I give the story a 7/10, the performances a 8/10 and the Cinematography a 9/10.

raw_payload.author: CamilaKrauss
raw_payload.content:
Chaos at its best. The film throws you into a whirlwind of noise, energy, and raw emotion. The music, for me, is a driving force behind it all, adding layers of tension and rhythm that keep you at the tip of your seat for the full 160 minutes, the piano in the second act standing out as unforgettable.

Surprisingly, it made me laugh out loud and truly care for the characters. At times, you even feel tenderness for "the bad ones," which is not easy to achieve. Beyond the chaos, it beautifully shows the strength and deep desire of a father and daughter to find each other again, and how, despite all the baggage one carries, love proves to be even bigger.

A sharp and resonant picture for these times.

raw_payload.author: A_FORTY_SEVEN
raw_payload.content:
My Rating : 6/10

'One Battle After Another' is Paul Thomas Anderson's 10th feature film and having directed such great films like 'There Will Be Blood' and 'The Master' expectations will definitely be high.

It reminds one of Coen Brothers and Quentin Tarantino however doesn't quite reach the same heights. The plot is very basic and the drama and chaos is far less interesting than can be expected of PTA. Seems superficial and parts unfinished, falls short in my opinion.

raw_payload.author: efd-10467
raw_payload.content:
A really interesting film, the first 10 minutes had me a bit lost, then it started to fall together.

Even though fantasy the parallels to modern America are clear, albeit exaggerated. DiCaprio was excellent, I'd say one of his best performances, the rest of the cast and production values are good as well.

It reminded of Pulp Fiction but on one story, in many ways an old fashion fun movie, but I bet many Americans don't enjoy seeing themselves portrayed like this, it's a little honest.

The cars and road scenes towards the end are epic, it built tension well, I haven't seen topography used like that to create atmosphere since 60's or 70's films, clever film making.

It is escapism with a touch of modern America.

raw_payload.author: andrewmonedero
raw_payload.content: If you're a PTA fan, definitely go watch this! If not, be prepared for a 2.5hr snooze fest. It does have a solid plot with an amazing villain, but that's about it. The characters that are supposed to be our heroes are not trustworthy and have motives I couldn't get behind. Overall, it was a beautiful film that I'll probably never see again.

raw_payload.author: snoducky
raw_payload.content:
A great depiction of power throughout the Americas.

This movie although long was enjoyable to watch all throughout. Dicaprio acting was brilliant, posing as realistic and engaging, he had moments of deep emotion and moments filled with action and tension. The cinematography is consistently dynamic and colourful alongside the script which is some of the most accurate to real life scripting i've ever seen in a movie. Plot wise the movie is very unique, it doesn't have a linear plot often showing perspectives of multiple characters yet in the end it all ties together to reveal how power is a totally hierarchical concept with even the most powerful leaders being under threat by other forms of their own power. Overall a bit long but worth the watch.

raw_payload.author: timmyhollywood
raw_payload.content:
There's a well known movie about a father rescuing his daughter called Taken. That movie probably runs 1 hr 30 mins to 1 hr 45 mins. At 3 hrs, Paul Thomas Anderson's One Battle After Another is like Taken if it was about revolutionaries, strange sexual proclivities, and a hero withOUT a "particular set of skills" but a burnout's lack of direction and short temper.

It's probably all allegorical, but that's not the kind of film that engages this viewer. I need to be immersed in narrative, swept up in action and/or emotion, and OBAA held me at a distance. The entire first act is mostly unnecessary, the second act is loooong, the third act has some moments, but we never really get to know these characters, or quite understand their fight, or feel grounded in any sense of realism.

From the first big scene with "Perfidia Beverly Hills" telling Sean Penn to "get up," and him then making a tent in his pants, we know this isn't going to be a realistic picture. Some viewers can still engage with something that simultaneously courts and denies verisimilitude, just not this one. The whole thing felt like a film to me, indeed a "PT Anderson Picture," and tbh I haven't really liked anything of his since There Will Be Blood (which was brilliant.)

Obviously, many people love his work and love this movie - I say good for you. I wish I could have enjoyed it too, instead of looking at my watch and waiting for it to be over.

See you on down the road.

6/10.

raw_payload.author: milosev80
raw_payload.content:
It's difficult to know where to begin with One Battle After Another. The film clearly wants to say something grand about justice, identity, and resistance - but somewhere along the way, it forgets to be a film. What could have been a layered story about conflict and morality instead collapses into a checklist of modern ideological talking points.

The characters don't speak - they announce. Every scene feels like it was written to win social media approval rather than to build emotional or narrative depth. The result is an oddly hollow experience: technically polished, but soulless. The moral universe is drawn with a crayon - one side irredeemably evil, the other universally enlightened - leaving no room for nuance, empathy, or genuine human struggle.

Visually, it's competent. The cinematography and production design are the film's saving grace. But even that can't disguise the writing's heavy-handedness. Instead of earning emotion, it demands it. Instead of exploring ideas, it preaches them.

Ultimately, One Battle After Another is less a movie than a manifesto. It might resonate with those who watch cinema for affirmation, but for anyone who watches it for storytelling, it's an exhausting exercise in self-importance. One star feels generous.

raw_payload.author: MFC9
raw_payload.content:
I tried to watch this film not expecting too much, but the hype and mostly positive reviews overwhelmed my simple little brain. I'm certainly not disappointed, but also not dazzled. The film is highly enjoyable, darkly funny and very entertaining, but not the masterpiece I was longing for.

Leo plays an ex-revolutionary whose partner (ex-Mrs Iman Shumpert Teyana Taylor) has turned for the feds, leaving him to raise their daughter alone. Penn is the weird maniac obsessed with the turncoat and in turn wants to hunt down her now 16yo daughter.

The film is far too long, but races along at such a pace that the viewer is never bored. Penn and Leo are both desperately waging dual Oscar campaigns, but it is DiCaprio who delivers a wonderful performance, not far removed from Rick Dalton, but no less brilliant. 8/10.

raw_payload.author: TakeTwoReviews
raw_payload.content: I do like a film that drops you in cold. This one drops us at the US/Mexican border with Perfidia (Teyana Taylor) and Pat aka Bob (Leonardo DiCaprio). They're odd-couple revolutionary activists of the vigilante militant persuasion, raiding the border, releasing detainees, blowing stuff up and pointing guns at Colonel Steven J. Lockjaw (Sean Penn). This first encounter is just a teaser, quite literally. The sexual tension between the cast is disarming, everyone seems charged and ready. It's got a slick heist movie pace and aesthetic, but with a hefty dose of dark comedic grit, that just keeps getting dirtier. The playfulness is to be short lived. Perfidia is not here for games and her renegade actions soon turn everyone's lives upside down. This could really only be an American story, nowhere else would this level of extreme idolised rhetoric, disfunctional society and sanctioned violence be plausible. It's viscerally emotional stuff and whips along at a frenetic pace, centred around the definition of a bizarre love triangle. But boy does it keep you on your toes! Just when you think you've got the measure of its players and are ready to see which direction they go, Director and Writer Paul Thomas Anderson throws more fuel on the fire. White supremacy, spurned lovers, race wars, hardline conservatism and conspiracy paranoia. It's a snake pit, a story that winds so tight, that when it uncoils, it explodes with a wildly weaving story arc, delivered with sledgehammer subtlety. It's brilliant. I mean it could easily drown under the chaos and weight of its narrative, the dizzying set piece sequences, the stars! DiCaprio is fantastic, Penn is even better, then we meet Sensei Carlos (Benicio Del Toro). Names you'll recognise, but Chase Infiniti (great name) as the young Willa is wonderful. Everyone is nuts, there's not one single likeable character. This would usually be a deal breaker for me. No connection, no empathy, no point. But this works and you know what almost steals all the limelight... a road, a bloody road! True it's the camerawork, the score and the edit that make the finale tick so well, but this is a rare example of a sprawling, wide eyed, dangerously ambitious film, that really lands.

raw_payload.author: macChocolate_3_16
raw_payload.content: ONE BATTLE AFTER ANOTHER (OBAA) Lots of things to be notice about this film it keeps a good hold on the viewer via script, remarkable acting by the actors. Actors expression and action are well used by the whole crew of OBAA their looks,color used by the editor,location,music .These things grab your attention throughout the hours. Okay let's talk about the OBAA script it takes you on a ride where a new location new character will be introduced it takes time to start but the journey of OBAA is worth watching great character Bob, willa , sensei, perfidia, Tim, antagonist colono lockjaw. Music is good it is absolutely fine. This is what I have to say about OBAA. Out of 10 i will go with 8 because sometimes some of the aspects do not match with each other basically during the first half of the movie But it is a magnificent craft of the makers it has everything as i said before music, location, character cast well directed fully made it worth watching.

raw_payload.author: jesspat3
raw_payload.content:
Ambitious, but not quite what I expected. The film weaves together a multitude of themes, at times harmonized and at others chaotic, much like everything else it portrays. This unevenness may feel disorienting, yet it is precisely where the film's beauty lies.

The performances are strong, with convincing and nuanced acting throughout.

raw_payload.author: BA_Harrison
raw_payload.content:
Paul Thomas Anderson's latest film deals with the subject of far-left activists (terrorists) doing battle with the far-right (an evil secret organisation), making it feel very topical. What it isn't, though, is a film worthy of all of the glowing reviews and high ratings that it has been receiving. Don't get me wrong... I didn't hate it, but this isn't the 10/10 masterpiece I had been led to believe it was.

Leonardo DiCaprio plays Bob, a political rebel whose past catches up with him, putting his daughter Willa (Chase Infiniti) at risk. Sean Penn is Col. Steven J. Lockjaw, whose ambition to become a member of the white supremacist Christmas Adventurers Club leads him to take drastic actions.

While the film definitely has its moments -- most notably some very dark comedy -- it isn't the relentlessly crazy action caper that it is clearly intended to be -- there's too much filler, the pace isn't as breakneck as it needs to be, and the film expects the viewer to side with Bob, despite his character being a violent revolutionary and, later in the film, a drunk and a stoner. The soundtrack is also awful -- horrible plinkety-plonkety jazz music playing over almost every scene. A lot of praise has been heaped upon Infiniti, and her performance is pretty good, but for me it is Penn who makes the film worth watching: his degenerate character is hilarious, with his awkward posture and weird facial tics.

If you're not deterred by the film's leftist political stance (and the terrible music), then there's a good chance you'll have a good time, but don't expect anything mind-blowing, 'cos it's not. 6/10.

raw_payload.author: kmkevinn-66699
raw_payload.content: According to Metacritic of best films of this year, with a 95% high a tie of "Kill Bill The Whole Bloody Affair" this film is 1st of its best films of this year by metacritic and this very well be definitely nominated awards into the coming months ahead of MMXXVl. Good story, one of DiCaprio's best performances in a while since 2016. He will be nominated in the award season for sure.

raw_payload.author: watcher-13

From random jazz music to what sounded like a kid just hitting a pot with a spoon repeatedly. During the car chase at the end, where they're going flat out trying to catch someone, why in the hell does the sound track repeatedly have them changing gears like they had stopped and are getting back up to speed?!?!? raw_payload.author: dim-78

Feels like the intention was not to be seen as a movie but to be read as a manual.

raw_payload.author: rangil-44228

The good: Pace and cinematography.

Important to say: I feel like this film is directed only to the American audience. As a non-American I don't know the political situation. So the film lacks a moral foundation for me to connect with.

raw_payload.author: robertschalken
raw_payload.content:
Movie is entertaining, and had me hooked from the intro. Seam Penn is a convincing villain, and he really carries this movie.

But the near constant piano was really wearing on me which made it hard to watch. Same with Dicrapio's constant nervous performance, he played it well, but it was tiresome..

raw_payload.author: danamcrane
raw_payload.content:
One Battle After Another pits two secret societies against each other: the revolutionary French 75, which relies on indoctrinating the public versus the Christmas Adventurers' white supremacists who leverage the power of the state.

As you might expect, it's not a fair fight.

After all, violence is the language of the state and while some may be able to briefly interrupt the dialog, the state always has the final say. But Paul Thomas Anderson reminds us that despite knowing we cannot win, there are those that have no choice but to fight anyway. Such is the case with Perfidia and her daughter Charlene.

While this may seem depressing, Anderson smartly focuses the movie on the bumbling Bob (Leonardo DiCaprio) who adds much needed levity to the seriousness of fascism in action. His is the role of the rest of us: providing the unconditional love to those that must fight, validating their struggle.

A key piece of the magic on screen here is the "lost in time" feel to the movie, which purports to be book-ended by Charlene's 16 years but actually spans the revolutionary movements and TV show references of the 60's/70's through the cell phones and caged immigrant visuals of the 90's/2000's. This quandary lends the movie a timelessness that makes it even more pertinent to today's military-supported ICE raids across major US cities.

If you like your humor black and your social commentary pulled from today's headlines, you've come to the right place.

raw_payload.author: jfernandez-01775
raw_payload.content: Great movie and story line. Relatable to what is going on in society today. Suspensful, star studded cast. Over two and a half hours long but well worth it. If you don't get to see it at the theater, this movie is worth renting or buying and watching it in the comfort of your own home with surround sound.

raw_payload.author: heatherklossner
raw_payload.content:
It may be labeled an action movie, but it isn't, and it's not a comedy either. It resists neat categorization, which is precisely the point. What it offers instead is an uncompromising piece of directorial vision: coherent, deliberate, and unsettlingly relevant in a way that feels less allegorical than observational.

There is action here, but not the variety driven by gunfire or body counts. The tension lives in movement, composition, editing, and sound-in how the camera reveals a world already in motion. The extended middle sequence built around the ICE raid functions as the film's nerve center: a massive, intricately choreographed depiction of collective response. Information moves hand to hand and frequency to frequency; skaters relay sightings, radio broadcasts redirect protesters, emergency protocols activate to relocate migrants. It's exhilarating precisely because it's procedural, communal, and frighteningly plausible. The filmmaking is confident enough to let the machinery run.

Despite its subject matter, the film is often very funny, almost entirely due to casting and performance rather than plot. DiCaprio leans into another stretched-thin, morally compromised role. Sean Penn, with his militaristic body control and facial tics telegraphing barely contained psychosis, is both unnerving and bizarre in a way that somehow works. The "Christmas Adventurers Club" lands as sharply satirical, and Chase Infiniti is the standout surprise, bringing a grounded presence that makes it feel like you're watching a career ignite in real time.

For me, this is the movie of the year. I only wish I'd seen it in a theater instead of streaming it at home.

raw_payload.author: AudioFileZ
raw_payload.content: I think this film was supposed to be an out-there novel made to celluloid, OK. If it was supposed to create a kind of sympathy for certain factions and a distrust for another, well it failed as it feels fictitiously hollow. I'll give it suspense and some decent acting in its fabrications. Blending truths with deceptions is a tricky path when played straight, so, not much except for the suspense and action that was well orchestrated for entertainment using several A-Listers. Just don't be fooled into thinking this is something that really happened like it attempts to play out. That has to be taken into consideration ratings wise. I give it 6.5 based on the whole.

raw_payload.author: dolphiea
raw_payload.content: This film is all over the place-- for a variety of reasons. Some fun, some overwrought. Everyone that has lived through the last 6 or so decades in America will recognize the 70's, 80's and 2020's political inferences. Intelligent...yes, but kind of indulgent and self serving. The Lockjaw character was cartoonish. Bob- hapless and kind of frantically irritating. Loved Sensei Sergio! And the end song--Tom Petty's American Girl- bloody perfect!

raw_payload.author: thePopcornExplorer
raw_payload.content:
I am glad I have started some time ago to watch movie from other countries and more independent ones as well because it does seem like Hollywood (with very few exceptions) has simply become a shadow of its former self.

In a sea of remakes and super heroes, some movies appear here and there with interesting new approaches on recurring themes or some great entertainment value, but in the rare occasions where it's not a sequel or a super hero movie you find some "ok" work that you would hope had more impact - that's where "One Battle After Another" comes in.

Sure it's well directed, great shots, contrary to some people I kind of liked the score, good action but the story is very basic. You can see the themes of revolution, generational struggles, activism and far right movements depicted but I mean they were just a plot device, it's not a political movie in the sense that it goes deeper into these topics, it's a good action movie that happens to have a superficial layer of statements.

It's a story that could've been told in way less time as well and the characters, maybe they did this for comedic effect, are a bit cartoonish at times, really exaggerated or with heavy stereotypes.

Good acting overall specially Chase Infiniti, a new kid on he block with great potential.

Lastly on a personal note, while I do find DiCaprio a great actor I sometimes struggle to separate the artist and the person (I know I should) and contrary to many criminals that these days are seeing justice brought to them, he hasn't been accused of any misconduct, however, and while I don't actively see pink magazines or am particularly interested in an actors's life, the whole dating 20 year old models is starting to become creepy. It was funny and it has been dealt with with humor many times but when I saw his scenes with Chase I was just thinking that in real life this would be an example of someone he dates and it just gave me the ick.

But again, long story short it's an ok movie, with good direction, really hyped, you won't miss to much if you don't watch it.

raw_payload.author: RandomArt
raw_payload.content: Merry Christmas and yes that's a scarier adventure club than the vigilante. Watch it to know the scary elite slave-lords of the current supremacy. Super awesome performances by all. The ending seems like an unrealistic setup but all along, it feels like a film with conviction and fantastic noir of a drama.

raw_payload.author: MM-006
raw_payload.content:
The worst movie I have ever watched. And it's not a comedy! I left depressed. We along with two more couples left before it ended.

I'm very disappointed in so many actors that I love making this movie. There were many different ways to bring about their message. However, a lesson to be learned here. Not every labeled "comedy" is truly a comedy. Don't be fooled.

raw_payload.author: joebloggscity
raw_payload.content:
I just can't believe I let this film pass me by on its release. Having felt wholly underwhelmed this past year on new cinema releases, I am so relieved and happy to have managed to finally catch this in the cinema in a year end retro showing. 'One Battle After Another' is a truly exceptional film on a par with the best of all films made so far this century in my opinion.

Taking in the underlying storyline arcs, I will leave all political analysis to a more appropriate forum than here. There's no need to analyse any of it here, and in any case the film takes stabs at people from all corners.

The film centres round a father and daughter in hiding only for the pursuit to reignite after 16 years. The father & his partner were members of some militant anti establishment revolutionary group, with his past catching up with him as an old demented sociopathic adversary out to find him & his daughter with his wife long since estranged. The father is a burnt-out stoner getting by intoxicated on drink & drugs, whilst his adversary is a sadistic machine.

Despite all the dark undertones, there's still a lot of dark humour, emotion and action. The film sees us follow the protagonists in the underbelly of society as the film becomes a Cat & Mouse chase, and we laugh & cry with them even when we may not approve of everything.

I can't recommend this film highly enough. When the awards season arrives I would be disheartened if this film doesn't get the honours it deserves. Its narrative is paced wonderfully, the action flows without being too heavy, and there's a perfect ensemble who are acting at the top of their game. Sean Penn steals the show as the sadistic antagonist whilst DiCaprio is excellent against type as the out of his depth father-cum-revolutionary in hiding.

It's fun, it's crazy yet still poignant & interesting. Please go out & see it.

raw_payload.author: ryan-28337
raw_payload.content: I really liked the movie, but the background music is terrible. Constant one key on the piano, takes away from really enjoying the movie. Other than that, everyone had a great proformance. The little bits of comedy were great. The sense of urgency kept you on the edge of your seat. Just once again. The piano in the background was awful.

raw_payload.author: Omicron_202
raw_payload.content: It takes a while to get into the movie, with some scenes not really making much sense at the beginning. But keep with it past the 30 minute mark. I found this movies totally engaging, with really great performances from the main actors and a heart tugging storyline. I really don't know why I've seen reviews of this movie saying it was a flop. Maybe due to overspending on production? But thats no reason to hate on this. Sit back, watch di caprio and the rest of the cast do their thing and enjoy a movie which i put on par with No Country For Old Men. I know they are worlds apart but I feel the same intensity of what's going on in their situation, and they both have you puckering your starfish with anticipation. Great movie!! Watch it ASAP:)

raw_payload.author: janasorl
raw_payload.content: One Battle After Another is a great movie -maybe not as groundbreaking as some claim (did anyone even watch Andor?), but if it helps more people understand how polarized the U. S. is and the internal decay under the current leadership, then all the better. I genuinely enjoyed the entire film: it has a nice flow, a gripping storyline that keeps you on the edge of your seat, brilliant performances, and a rewarding finale. This one is definitely Oscar-worthy.

raw_payload.author: noawareness
raw_payload.content:
Undoubtedly a great film with some incredible performances but it's just not THAT good. The story is a little contrived and tries a little too hard to be what it so desperately wants to be but just comes off as pretentious. It's so "of its time" that it's painful and it will probably be dated by next year.

That said, the acting is amazing, it's filmed as diligently as you'd expect and the characters/development are fantastic.

A great film that isn't amazing and I'll probably never watch it again.

The problem isn't the film, it's the people who overrate it that are the problem.

raw_payload.author: DaneliusUK
raw_payload.content:
Well, what can I say?

One Battle After Another is a gritty, dark film driven by intense momentum and complex characters.

One standout moment is the use of the Mandala Throws in a couple of shots. While the creative intent isn't entirely clear, they seem to suggest cycles, repetition, and the idea that every battle inevitably feeds into the next.

If you're a film junkie like myself then you'll appreciate the time and effort that Paul Thomas Anderson and everyone else put in to make this happen.

I enjoyed it. Not my place to say where it could have been improved. It is what it is.

Thank you.

raw_payload.author: geeeneralmarshall
raw_payload.content: After all the 10/10 reviews, I had really high expectations, which unfortunately weren't met in the end. Technically perfect, no question, but unfortunately it didn't manage to move me emotionally. Many storylines were started but not finished, and the ending is also unsatisfying. It's a real shame, because I feel that the film actually had incredible potential, but a lot of it was left untapped. At the same time, I have to say that I find it incredibly difficult to rate this film, partly because I think it was advertised incorrectly. If you watch the trailer, 90% of it is from the first 20 minutes, after which it unfortunately becomes something completely different. If you go in expecting it to be a comedy action thriller, it's certainly a very good film, but if you go in expecting the political thriller/drama promised in the trailer, then unfortunately it's only a 6.5/10, which is what I'm giving the film here.

raw_payload.author: taoistview
raw_payload.content:
One Battle After Another doesn't play by the usual Hollywood rules. This isn't your typical action movie with explosions and predictable thrills . It's raw, tense, and surprisingly human. Every chase, fall, and fight feels grounded, messy, and real.

The story itself isn't new: a dad pulled back into a world he thought he'd left behind. But the way it's done makes it hit harder than you'd expect. The camera gets right up close ,shaky, sometimes almost uncomfortable and it really makes you feel what the characters are going through. What I loved most is its honesty. It doesn't glamorize violence or pretend sacrifice is romantic. It just shows the cost.

DiCaprio is exactly what you want here: weathered, vulnerable, and full of those quiet moments where silence says more than words. The pacing is uneven on purpose chaotic at times, mirroring his state of mind. Some people might find it jarring, but for me, it works.

At the end of the day, One Battle After Another is more than an action movie. It's about exhaustion, responsibility, and the painful clarity that comes with loss. It's not easy to watch, it's bruising , but that's exactly why it sticks with you. Honestly, it's better than most of the movies I've seen recently.

raw_payload.author: dugrand_cafe25
raw_payload.content: There's many good things about how PTA filmed this BUT...the story falls apart when a key characters' actions are misaligned with their backstory/personality. Leo's girlfriend gets pregnant (not a spoiler, it's in the previews) but why she keeps it? She's a revolutionary that supports abortion. You'll understand my pov at the end of the film.

raw_payload.author: erick-hernandez
raw_payload.content:
There are bad movies, there are forgettable movies-and then there is One Battle After Another. A cinematic endurance test disguised as "comedy" and "adventure," this nearly three-hour detour into despair felt less like an evening at the cinema and more like a hostage situation where the ransom note was the script.

Leonardo DiCaprio stars, though "stars" here is generous. His performance is like watching a Ferrari forced to tow a shopping cart uphill-an expensive engine wasted on the wrong job. DiCaprio has proven himself in great films, but here, he's reduced to little more than a highly paid tour guide through a swamp of half-baked jokes and scenes that stretch on with all the comedic precision of a tax seminar.

The director clearly believed they were making a clever satire. What they actually delivered was a two-hour-and-fifty-minute inside joke that never lands, performed with the solemnity of a Shakespearean tragedy. Imagine someone insisting they're hilarious while reading you a phone book in a dimly lit basement. That's the "comedy."

The adventure element? Unless you count the adventure of trying to escape the theater without offending friends who dragged you there, none was detected. Instead, what unfolds is a gloomy parade of clichés, shot with the self-importance of a documentary about rare moss.

Entertainment this is not. In fact, it does the opposite: it drains you. I left the theater feeling not just bored but somehow wronged, as though I'd been promised a fine dinner and served a damp napkin. By the end, I was telling my wife I would have preferred three hours of watching YouTube videos about antique lamps-or even, dare I say, instructional content on carburetor repair. At least those would have provided genuine value.

As for the director, if this was their vision of humor and adventure, one can only hope their next project is less ambitious. Perhaps a silent meditation retreat. With no cameras.

Verdict: One Battle After Another is not a movie-it's a cautionary tale. Save your ticket money, invest in a documentary about garden gnomes, and thank me later.

raw_payload.author: IvanBaz97
raw_payload.content: One Battle After Another is a very unusual and engaging film with an interesting story and unexpected humor. Leonardo DiCaprio delivers a strong performance, though his character feels intentionally passive - he doesn't really manage or control the main events, which made me question the idea behind his characterization. Sean Penn plays a character you wouldn't want to know or meet in real life, yet he feels disturbingly realistic, reminding us that such people live among us. raw_payload.author: BoBo_Goal32
raw_payload.content:
Luckly, I haven't read a word about this movie before watching it. Came fresh and got what I knew I am going to get - a Paul Thomas Anderson's film, with all the elements that are tied to this director's creations. He is not my favorite director, and I'm pretty divided regarding his filmography.

One thing that I can say about Paul Thomas Anderson is that his movies stick. Makes me think and are almost always enjoyable. Well, not all of them. There are "Inherent Vice" that made me suffer and also "The Master" I didn't like. And yes, Paul Thomas Anderson's films are always long.

This one is also a long one and not necessarily needed to be that long, but it is one of those things I can live with, because this time his movie is absolutely great. It is not great like the movie critics say it is. It's not perfect and sometimes it feels too stuffed with allegories and subjects that Paul Thomas Anderson tried to get inside, but other then all of those...yeah...it's that good.

It is like America's political atmosphere started to use drugs, from both kinds. Downers and uppers. There are two main characters that are performed by Leonardo DiCaprio and Sean Penn and are representing each one of drugs types. One is pumped and violent and the other is a suspicious stoner, that is sometime very mellow and sometimes paranoic.

Other then that Paul Thomas Anderson talks about America, about treatment to foreigners and especially those who tries to blend in America. He speaks about the fact we all belong to places, culture and personal identity we get from our family and our close (and also not that close) environment.

Everything Paul Thomas Anderson does in this movie is fabulous. Camera work is sensational, the musical score is almost perfect and stressful, and the final car chase scene is one of the best thing I've seen in any movie lately. The best part, off course, will be the actors choices.

What more can we say about Leonardo DiCaprio and his performances? Nothing else. All was already been told. He is perfect. Give him another Oscar. Sean Penn is absolutely terrifying in one of his best performances of latest years. Benicio Del Toro maintained his acting vibe from 'The Phoenician Scheme' and young Chase Infiniti stole everyone's thunder.

This movie gets the jog done. It is interesting, well made and acted and especially very unique, though also very stuffed. I am so happy I managed to enjoy this one without caving into the hype machine around the web. Is stood by itself and made my experience just great and it is another one by PTA that I will remember as one of the better ones.

Not flawless, but still thumping entertainment.

raw_payload.author: franx-1
raw_payload.content:
First off, Leo was magnetic-as was Penn, who seems incapable of phoning it in unless the phone happens to be on fire. The rest of the cast, though, looked like they'd been assembled from the lost-and-found at central casting. The movie had real potential, but instead of trimming the fat, they seemed to add extra marbling for texture. Entire scenes felt like cinematic gristle.

I kept glancing at the clock, willing the hands to move faster, only to be ambushed by the occasional dazzling action sequence. It was like being bored at a dinner party until the host suddenly brings out flamethrowers.

In the end, the film mimicked PTA's career: dazzling highs, baffling lows, sometimes both in the same minute. The closing car chase, though-sharp, suspenseful, the kind of scene that almost made me forget I'd been counting ceiling tiles an hour earlier. If the movie had been that taut all along, we might be calling it a masterpiece. Instead, we're calling it: "Well, at least the ending didn't suck."

raw_payload.author: cinemasauron
raw_payload.content:
Paul Thomas Anderson's 10th feature film may as well be his most amusing & entertaining one to date. An incendiary adventure that comes brimming with a charged atmosphere, laced with rebellious spirit, packed with relevant themes and bolstered by a potent ensemble, One Battle After Another incorporates much of what's happening in present-day America to tell its tale of defiance & resistance.

Also written by Anderson (Phantom Thread & Licorice Pizza), the story concerns a group of ex-revolutionaries who reunite to rescue the daughter of one of their own when their past nemesis resurfaces. Volatile from the get-go, the film gathers momentum right away while exhibiting an undercurrent of tension & urgency that the picture not only sustains with effortless ease but also sprinkles with hilarious bits along the way.

The technical aspects are sound throughout, be it the steady camerawork, smooth pace, neat editing, interesting score & some expertly choreographed action set pieces. It is also an acting powerhouse, with the entire cast contributing with excellent inputs. Leonardo DiCaprio renders his role with stoned perfection and he's brilliantly supported by Sean Penn's intense showcase, Benicio del Toro's zen act & Chase Infinity's fiery performance.

Overall, One Battle After Another is crafted with controlled aggression, unfolds with a sense of chaos & emergency and delivers a thoroughly fun ride despite its politically & thematically rich layerings. Presenting the esteemed auteur in complete control of his craft and also impressive enough to rank amongst the best all-round films of the year, Anderson's latest feature further affirms his status as one of the preeminent filmmakers of his generation.

All the actors, every last one of them, do great work.

The cinematography is especially strong. You may have heard about the camera work during the chase sequence near the end - and especially on IMAX, this is excellent technical work. And probably not for the reasons you think.

I'm a fan of PTA and the 60s/70s films he's referencing here. I'm politically aligned with his message. I like strident piano music.

But this is a deeply unpleasant film that doesn't soar very high and then unravels in the final acts. He sets up political allegories and then resolves them with a chase & revenge arc. Like every Hollywood superhero and action movie. It's a boring trope that collapses the cliches onto itself.

raw_payload.author: Tweekums
raw_payload.content:
As this film starts a group of far left radicals known as 'French 75' target a US Immigration detention centre; they free the detainees but that isn't all. One of then, a woman known as Perfidia Beverly Hills sexually humiliates the commanding officer, Steven J. Lockjaw. Lockjaw manages to catch her at a later date and pressures her into having sex with him. Perfidia is involved with fellow revolutionary Pat Calhoun and when she has a baby, Charlene, Pat thinks it is time to settle down but she continues and is caught. Lockjaw arranges for her to be freed into witness protection if she names everybody else involved. Pat manages to get away with Charlene; with new names: Bob and Willa. Sixteen years pass and Bob is a stoner living in a remote corner of California with Willa. Lockjaw, now a colonel is invited to join the 'Christmas Adventurers'; a powerful and shadowy white suprematist group... they wouldn't be impressed to learn of his 'indiscretion' with Perfidia; more because she is black than her criminal past, especially given that he might be Willa's father. He determines to find and if necessary eliminate her. Bob and Willa's lives are about to be turned upside down as they are forced to go on the run and rely on old comrades.

I thought this was a solid film; it provided plenty of solid action as well as a few good laughs. Most of the characters are morally ambiguous and several are obviously just bad. They also feel slightly exaggerated in a good way. The cast does a fine job bringing their characters to life; especially Sean Penn who is delightfully over the top as Lockjaw. The film looks good; the VistaVision is used to full effect in the desert scenes; especially a car chase where the camera is almost at road level. The action is nicely varied and suitably exciting. On the down side it is a bit too long; the story doesn't really justify over two and a half hours. Overall I'd still say this is worth watching.

raw_payload.author: KamyarMatthew
raw_payload.content:
Every victory costs a piece of the soul and some men keep paying.

One Battle After Another unfolds like a confession whispered in the dark , a reminder that the fiercest wars are the ones nobody sees. This is a story built on psychological shrapnel, emotional trench lines, and two men who carry their ghosts like armor.

Leonardo DiCaprio moves through the frame with that haunted precision he saves for characters who've already lost something essential. He doesn't perform pain , he inhales it, keeps it under his skin, lets it leak through the smallest flicker in his eyes. There's a quiet madness there, the kind you only notice if you've lived long enough to recognize the same fracture in yourself.

Sean Penn answers him like a shadow answers a flame not competing, but completing. His presence feels weathered, wounded, deeply human. Penn doesn't act the battles; he wears them. Every scene with him feels like a man negotiating with the universe, hoping it gives him a different answer this time, even if he knows it won't.

The film's power isn't in the plot ,it's in the spaces between breaths, in the way guilt and longing twist together until you can't tell them apart. It's a meditation on persistence, on the price of survival, and on the strange truth that some people fight because they don't know what life feels like without a war.

If you've ever lived through your own quiet disasters, this film hits like a mirror you weren't ready to face.

And if you're lucky enough to watch it at the right moment, it might feel like someone finally named the storm inside you.


* Kamyar Matthew
"I write where films stop and the soul begins."

raw_payload.author: MrDHWong
raw_payload.content:
"One Battle After Another" is an action thriller film loosely based on the 1990 novel "Vineland" by Thomas Pynchon. Written and directed by Paul Thomas Anderson ("Boogie Nights", "Magnolia", "There Will Be Blood") and starring Leonardo DiCaprio, Sean Penn, and Benicio del Toro, it is a fast-paced, farcical experience that appears to attack both ends of the political spectrum.

In 2009 in Los Angeles, the far-left revolutionary group "French 75" has been responsible for many dangerous incidents, including breaking out detained immigrants, bombing government buildings, and robbing banks. Two of the group's core members, Pat Calhoun (Leonardo DiCaprio) and Perfidia Beverly Hills (Teyana Taylor), begin to develop romantic feelings for one another, culminating in the birth of a baby girl they name Charlene. After Perfidia abandons her parental responsibilities to continue fighting in the revolution, Pat and Charlene are forced to go on the run, assuming the new identities of Bob and Willa Ferguson. Sixteen years later, Bob has retired from French 75 and has become a cannabis-addicted loner, holding the now-teenaged Willa (Chase Infiniti) to a protective standard. One day, Bob and Willa find themselves in the sights of corrupt far-right military officer Steven Lockjaw (Sean Penn), a longtime enemy of French 75 who has been pursuing the pair ever since they went into hiding years earlier. Realising their lives are now in danger, Bob hastily comes out of retirement to rejoin French 75 to prevent Lockjaw from tracking him and Willa down by any means necessary.

I can't say I'm too familiar with the works of Thomas Pynchon, but from what I have read he sounds like a big deal in the literary world. Upon researching him further, it would appear that many of the stories in Pynchon's bibliography tend to focus around a variety of topics in the realm of postmodernism and pop culture as a whole. Though I haven't read any of his books, from personal experience my first exposure to Thomas Pynchon was his two appearances on "The Simpsons", where he is seen wearing a paper bag over his head, something particularly unusual considering how notoriously reclusive he is in real life. Following the 2014 film adaptation of "Inherent Vice", Pynchon's 1990 novel "Vineland" has now inspired 2025's "One Battle After Another", a quick-cut, erratic movie that features some visceral action, creative cinematography, and some darkly comical moments that seem to be very much in line with the author's unique style.

Admittedly, I wasn't too fond of the way the film was setting things up at first, but as the story pressed on things started to make more sense to me. Opening on a group of young group of people congregating at the US-Mexico border, we are shown the members of French 75, a bunch of far-left wannabe revolutionaries plotting their next plan of attack; freeing all of the detained refugees locked up at customs. The two most prominent members of French 75, Pat and Perfidia, are seen in a romantic relationship with each other, clearly bonding over their own perverted sense of justice breaking and entering into a prison to free undocumented immigrants. Afterwards, we see these two participating in various other illegal activities masquerading as activism, like sneaking into political establishments to plant bombs and holding people at gunpoint at banks to rob them of their money. Though I initially thought the film was glorifying these actions as "cool" and "edgy" for entertainment's sake, the more I thought about it, the more I realised it is actually satirising the over-the-top manner in which these people likely view themselves, essentially painting them as arrogant and reckless terrorists doing more harm than good.

This then leads into the way the film handles another key character; Colonel Steven J. Lockjaw. Unlike those in French 75, Lockjaw's views are far-right leaning, with his ultimate goal being that he hopes to join a prominent white supremacist group, something complicated by his sexual preference for black women. It is worth noting that Lockjaw harbours erotic thoughts for Perfidia, who had an intimate encounter with him sixteen years earlier, which may or may not have resulted in him being the father of her half-breed child. Since the white supremacists will not allow a member with an interracial kid, Lockjaw sets out to pursue Perfidia's ex-partner Bob and his daughter Willa, utilising equally extreme tactics as French 75, like abusing his power to make his troops aggressively do most of his dirty work for him. What I enjoyed here was the way the film basically sends up the ridiculousness of the extreme ends of the political spectrum, being that both the left and the right are capable of dangerous things in the hands of those who misuse their influence.

Like with Pynchon's previous film adaptation "Inherent Vice", Paul Thomas Anderson helms another one of his stories with just as much creative care as last time. I was particularly taken in by the film's briskly-paced editing, which helps the story move along at such a comfortable rate that it's easy to forget it has a runtime of 162 minutes. There were quite a few times where I found myself invested in a scene simply because of how quickly it leads into the next one. For instance, during a scene where Bob's location is discovered by Lockjaw, he is seen escaping through a tunnel underneath his house, which is made difficult after a tear gas canister is thrown down there. The way Anderson holds on Bob's face as he desperately tries to make his way out of these cramped conditions with the gas hindering his progress kept me in suspense as to whether or not he will reach the end with or without some longterm damage to himself.

Yet despite the film's fast pacing, Anderson never forgets to keep the film as atmospheric as possible, reminding his audience that these are all gritty places inhabited with tangible people. Every location felt like it had personality, ranging from the harsh, unforgiving deserts to the convenient hideaway house in the woodlands that Bob and Willa call home. It also helps that Anderson uses his trademark long takes when establishing a setting. In the previously mentioned underground escape scene, Anderson holds on this for almost a whole minute to emphasise how far Bob needs to crawl his way to safety, showing us that if he stops for even a brief moment, this tunnel could end up being his tomb.

On that note, the film also contains some darkly comical humour from time to time, another one of Anderson's trademarks. Though I wouldn't exactly refer to this movie as a "comedy" in the traditional sense, I'll admit it did have me chuckling every now and then. For example, there is a running joke about how Bob has been out of the game for so long that he can no longer remember important code words when communicating with other members of French 75. This leads to Bob having some funny blow-ups on the phone directed at one member who insists he follow revolutionary protocol and deliver the exact code without any verbal errors. Moments like these actually felt earned throughout the story and not awkwardly inserted in for the sake of it.

Once again showing his versatility as an actor, Leonardo DiCaprio brings a particular level of vulnerability to the character of Bob (a.k.a. Pat). He plays him like someone who was considered a hero back in his heyday but is now nothing more than a washed-up loser in today's world. Though it is clear that Bob's best days are behind him, he sets all of that aside to keep Willa safe, especially once their old enemy Lockjaw rears his head again after so long. As to be expected by now, DiCaprio absolutely nails a role like this.

As for the rest of the cast, Sean Penn also stands out as Colonel Lockjaw, a character so driven by what he does and so committed to completing a job that it's hard not to feel a little scared every time he is on screen. This is someone who has built his entire reputation around his line of work, cold and calculated in the way he handles business. Other standouts include Chase Infiniti as Bob's daughter Willa and Benicio del Toro as Sergio St. Carlos, one of Bob's allies in the revolution. It's worth noting that none of the characters in the movie are likeable in any way, but they're all interestingly written enough to keep viewers like me invested in what happens to them by the end. Given how long we spend with each person throughout the film's long runtime, that is one of its biggest strengths.

Standing out as another winner from Paul Thomas Anderson, "One Battle After Another" proves itself to be one of the filmmaker's best movies in years. It's rare for such a long movie to go by so quickly, in fact, not since another Leonardo DiCaprio film, "The Wolf of Wall Street", have I seen such an occurence take place. Though I haven't read the original Thomas Pynchon story from which this is based upon, I have a feeling the reclusive author probably feels satisfied with the final product. At least I can only guess so at this point in time.

I rate it 8.5/10.
