I also felt like I was in a world I did not understand, e.g. The use of coronavirus like face masks but then in a time which felt much earlier than 2022. So I was confused about the timing. I also did not feel any strong emotional impact by the movie. The bad guys felt cliche bad guys, not like actual human beings. Also there were few parts that were funny but for a dark comedy not funny enough. Lenoardo is good actor as always. Sean Penn character I felt was badly written but I think he did good in the role. Supporting characters were overall fine. There were no bad performances. I don't think that Willa character was that well played though. Also I did not feel like I believed any of the characters. Visually the movie was good. I felt transported where the movie was taking place. Lots of movement in this movie but I felt imersed. I could feel the atmosphere. The scenes felt fien in rhythm, the scene transitions were too much in the start of the movie. Also many parts felt unbelievable or over the top. The first part of the movie was hard to follow. The second part was fine. Start too rushed. Honestly didn't pay attention to the score or soundtrack, but I think it was decent. Sound design and effects were fine, can't say anything bad about that. There were times were sound enhanced the scenes and there some good song choices. The production design and effects were good. Sets and costumes and props made sense, the movies was visually appealing, and the world didn't feel like our world, but if it was supposed to be an alternative world it felt fine. Overall the plot was not engaging and well-written, it did not flow naturally, the acting performances were fine but not super convincing, I didn't care at all about the characters except Sean Penns character Lockjaw, the movie looked visually good, good use of camera, colors and lighting, the movie felt too fast in the start and end, but middle was just right, soundtrack was okay, sets costumes effects fit in to the world.

raw_payload.author: PlushieCinemaBuddies
raw_payload.content:
Willow 🐉 picked One Battle After Another for this weekend's Plushie Crew movie epic, and it was the perfect choice for a first-time dive into Paul Thomas Anderson. None of the crew had seen a PTA film before, but hearing this one hailed as the best film of 2025-and seeing all the buzz about its mix of action, suspense, and moral dilemmas-made it impossible to skip. The cast felt instantly familiar, too: Leonardo DiCaprio (Winny 🐻 knew him from Titanic, even if he hadn't actually seen it), Benicio Del Toro (from The Phoenician Scheme), and Teyana Taylor (from Stray). Having such recognizable, talented actors made it feel like a major cinematic event for the crew.

The movie itself is a mix of adrenaline-pumping action and thoughtful character moments. The action sequences are intense and well-choreographed without being over-the-top, but it's the moral dilemmas and high-stakes choices that really give the story its weight. Characters constantly make tough or morally gray decisions, which keeps the story engaging-but also makes parts feel complex or messy. Some scenes drag or pile on too much, and the pacing can feel uneven, but there's almost always something compelling happening on screen.

Willow 🐉 was fully invested, tracking the plot closely and thinking through the characters' decisions. Winny 🐻 enjoyed the drama and character interactions, even if some of the suspenseful sequences made him a little nervous. Amy 🎀 loved following the emotional arcs and seeing how the characters evolved under pressure. Tails 🦊 was fascinated by the strategy, twists, and tactical aspects of the story, analyzing how the characters reacted under pressure. Sonic 🌀 was into the pace and excitement, while Kuromi 😈 and Mimikyu 👻 found fun and chaotic moments to latch onto. Sunny 🌞 and Sweet 🍓 were drawn to the big emotional beats, and Lala 🌸 appreciated the subtle artistry and visual storytelling.

The performances are one of the strongest points. DiCaprio brings vulnerability and determination that makes his character relatable even amidst chaos. Benicio Del Toro provides grounded intensity and occasional humor, balancing the tension. Teyana Taylor adds emotional depth that keeps her character fully realized. Chase Infiniti as Willa is compelling, giving the story heart alongside the main leads. Every actor contributes to making the messy or dense parts feel worth watching.

Cinematography and score are also highlights. The visuals are painterly and precise, framing both intimate character moments and large-scale action. The music enhances both tension and emotional resonance without overpowering the story. Some plot threads are dense, and the political themes can feel heavy-handed, but the craft and care in every scene make it engaging.

Overall, One Battle After Another is a movie that will leave mixed feelings-perfect for a Plushie Crew watch. It's ambitious, occasionally messy, and sometimes overwhelming, but it's also thrilling, emotionally resonant, and packed with memorable performances. It sparks discussion and debate, making it perfect for a group that loves analyzing character choices, story twists, and cinematic style. For the crew experiencing PTA for the first time, it's a wild, unforgettable ride that balances spectacle, moral weight, and heart.

Verdict: 8/10 - a thrilling, messy, and deeply human cinematic experience. It challenges, entertains, and rewards attention, and it's exactly the kind of film that makes a Saturday movie epic with the Plushie Crew feel special.

raw_payload.author: fredrikgunerius
raw_payload.content:
Paul Thomas Anderson's dystopian crime-action is an all-out assault on eyes, ears, and almost every other faculty - a film as rich and multifaceted as it is hyperbolic and self-absorbed. Leonardo DiCaprio plays bomb specialist Pat Calhoun, whose girlfriend, Perfidia Beverly Hills (Teyana Taylor), is a leading figure in a far-left revolutionary group determined to overthrow the right-wing militant government. When the two become parents to little Charlene (Chase Infiniti), and Perfidia rats out her comrades after being apprehended by law enforcement, led by Colonel Steven J. Lockjaw (Sean Penn), Pat's perspective shifts, and he leaves his old world behind - seemingly.

The aptly titled One Battle After Another may not exactly be a battle to watch, but it alternately gnaws at and imposes itself on you. At its best, such as in the tender portrayal of the bond between father and daughter (DiCaprio and Infiniti), the film encompasses universal truths and sensitive themes in a poignant package. In its more feverish parts, Anderson cannot quite mold his characters' overblown personas and his emblematic scenes into a fully reconciled satire to form the film's undercurrent. There are nevertheless flashes of brilliance throughout, highlighted by a delicately innovative, brilliantly shot chase sequence towards the end. The film is also somewhat marred by Jonny Greenwood's abrasive, insistent score. There are entertaining, occasionally baffling performances from the supporting cast - including a wild-looking Penn, the talented Infiniti, and an aptly laid-back Benicio del Toro - plus, of course, another commanding, accomplished turn from the ever-brilliant DiCaprio.

raw_payload.content: Going into this movie, I was going in blind and not much of a clue of what the plot of the movie is. I'm happy that was the case because this movie was a great surprise and had a lot of heart and soul put into it. Multiple of performances in this movie got me hooked and the story that made it much better. This movie's camera style was excellent and got me to be intrigued by what's going on in the exact scene. The character development, as crazy as the movie can be, is relatable and a joy to watch unfold, so you don't have to worry about the characters being unintelligent for no reason. Go watch this movie as soon as possible, it's absolutely worth watching.
